sourceFiles.name: thermal-system

fix.title: Lower cooling fan activation threshold below emergency shutdown threshold
fix.description: This fix removes the unreachable redundant behaviour by revising the cooling fan activation requirement and model attribute so the fan activates before emergency shutdown. Setting the fan start threshold to 75C preserves both behaviours in a reachable sequence: mitigation first, shutdown second if temperature continues to rise.

changes.description: Revised the cooling fan activation requirement threshold from 85C to 75C so it is reachable before emergency shutdown at 80C.
Updated the CoolingSystem fan start threshold attribute to match the revised requirement and eliminate the redundant unreachable state.

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model Characterisation
- System purpose: A simple thermal protection/control model intended to define temperature-triggered responses for a system, including emergency shutdown and a sprinkler subsystem.
- Operational domain: Industrial / facility or equipment thermal safety/control.
- Model scope: Small model fragment containing two requirement definitions and a minimal structural model.
- Known scope limitations: No behaviour model, no state machine, no verification model, no allocations, no interfaces, no packages beyond one top-level package, and no explicit requirement relationships. Checks that depend on those artefacts are assessed only against what is present and otherwise passed with scope rationale.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The two requirements have distinct trigger conditions and distinct intents ("emergency shutdown" at 80C vs "cooling fan" at 85C); no duplicated requirement text or repeated verification cases are present in the provided fragment.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  Only one defined part type relevant to thermal control (`CoolingSystem`) is present in the fragment, and no second near-identical `part def`/`item def` or duplicate interface type appears in the provided model text.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or repeated specialization relationships are present in this fragment, so no duplicated relationships were found.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  The model contains no state machines, actions, sequences, or message exchange behaviour, so there is no behavioural duplication to assess in the provided scope.

CHECK PASSED: 5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints  -  The attributes in `CoolingSystem` are distinct (`temperatureC`, `emergencyShutdownThresholdC`, `coolingFanStartThresholdC`) and do not restate each other; no repeated constraints or duplicated derived attributes are present.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  The decomposition shown is minimal and does not include pass-through intermediate blocks, empty packages, or single-subaction behavioural layers.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::emergencyShutdownThresholdC; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC
Check:          8. States that are never activated
Finding:        The model defines a cooling-fan activation condition at 85C while also defining emergency shutdown at 80C. If emergency shutdown stops normal operation at 80C as the requirement text states, the 85C fan activation condition becomes unreachable in operation and is therefore redundant.
Evidence:       Requirement text 1: "The system shall enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius." Requirement text 2: "The cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius." Attribute values: `attribute emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;` and `attribute coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 85.0;`
Score:          0.3  -  Significant defect because the model appears to define a higher-temperature response that cannot occur if shutdown is triggered first.
Confidence:     HIGH  -  The redundancy is directly supported by explicit threshold values and requirement text in the model.
Risk:           Analysts or implementers may assume the cooling fan provides mitigation above 85C when, under the stated shutdown logic, that condition is never reached in service. This can create false confidence in thermal control coverage and leave the model internally inconsistent.
Recommendation: Remove or revise the redundant fan-activation condition, or explicitly model the intended sequencing. If the fan is supposed to act before shutdown, lower `coolingFanStartThresholdC` below 80C and update `CoolingFanActivation`. If both are intentional for different modes, annotate that intent and add behaviour showing how 85C remains reachable.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated architectural elements suggesting intentional design redundancy were present; the only redundancy identified is logical/unreachable behaviour implied by conflicting thresholds, which is structural/requirements redundancy rather than deliberate fault-tolerance.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.3 | HIGH | Cooling fan activation at 85C appears redundant because emergency shutdown is required at 80C first. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.3
Model Status: Critical deficiencies - major rework required before this model can be used
